items.sourceNote: Inspired by 換日線 article on Japanese complaint-handling master — rewritten as an ironic 'smile is the cheapest armor' observation from the service worker's POV, original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
面試官：「請問你最大的優點是？」
我：「我會在被罵的時候微笑。」
面試官：「太好了，明天就來上班。」
我：「⋯⋯這真的算優點嗎？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 服務業徵才條件第一名是「抗壓性」，翻譯成白話就是「會在崩潰邊緣繼續笑」。傳給那位昨天面試完才發現自己被錄取真的不是好事的朋友。
variations: 「抗壓性高」是服務業最危險的讚美，等於說你還可以再被多踩一腳。
微笑是服務業最便宜的盔甲，所以公司才會發那麼多。

en.content:
Interviewer: "What's your greatest strength?"
Me: "I smile while being yelled at."
Interviewer: "Perfect. Start tomorrow."
Me: "...Wait, is that actually a strength?"
en.editorNote: The #1 service-industry hiring criterion is "stress tolerance," which translates to "can keep smiling on the edge of a breakdown." Send this to the friend who finished the interview and only later realized getting hired wasn't actually good news.
variations: "High stress tolerance" is the most dangerous compliment in service work — it means there's still room to step on you harder.
A smile is the cheapest armor in service work, which is why the company hands them out for free.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 經理人 2026 新竹奧客事件評論「寧可得罪奧客，也不要氣走員工」 management角度； rewritten from employee POV as a 「公司現在開始護員工，是因為徵不到下一個」 cynical reframe — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
以前主管說：「客人最大，先道歉再說。」
現在主管說：「不要忍，公司挺你。」
中間發生了什麼事？
中間發生的是——
他們發現徵不到下一個我了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近發現公司突然開始「保護員工」的同事。不是公司變好了，是缺工讓你變貴了。傳給對「公司新政策」抱持希望的菜鳥，溫柔提醒一下。
variations: 公司開始挺員工，不是因為良心發現，是因為履歷表變薄了。
「不要忍」的前提，是公司知道再徵一個比安撫客人還貴。

en.content:
Old manager line: "Customer is king. Apologize first, sort it out later."
New manager line: "Don't take it. We've got your back."
What happened in between?
In between, they figured out
they couldn't hire the next me.
en.editorNote: Send this to anyone who suddenly noticed the company is "protecting employees" now. The company didn't grow a conscience — the labor shortage made you expensive. A gentle reality check for newer coworkers who still believe in "new policies."
variations: Companies start protecting employees not because they found a conscience, but because the résumé stack got thin.
"Don't take it" only kicks in once the company realizes hiring your replacement costs more than soothing a complaint.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by BuzzFeed 2026-05-19 retail-worker wild-stories roundup; rewritten as a 「客人把店當自家客廳」 boundary-blurring joke — original phrasing avoiding direct anecdote copy

zh-tw.content:
客人坐在試衣間外面的沙發上，
脫掉鞋、滑手機、
叫他老公去拿衣服、
吃自己帶的麵包配可樂。
我走過去禮貌地問：「請問需要協助嗎？」
他抬頭看我：「妳很吵欸。」
抱歉，這是賣場，不是您家的二樓。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「客人把店當客廳」攻擊過的零售同事。我們提供更衣間、試穿椅、空調，但沒有附贈一個「家」的感覺給您。
variations: 客人把賣場當自己家，唯一沒有的是房貸壓力。
您在這裡可以試穿、可以休息，但不能脫鞋、不能野餐、不能罵店員。

en.content:
Customer sitting on the fitting-room couch:
shoes off, scrolling her phone,
ordering her husband to fetch outfits,
eating a sandwich she brought from home, washing it down with a Coke.
I walked over: "Can I help you with anything?"
She looked up: "You're being loud."
Ma'am, this is a department store. Not the second floor of your house.
en.editorNote: Send to retail coworkers recently ambushed by the "customer who treats the store like a living room" species. We provide fitting rooms, benches, air conditioning — not a complimentary feeling of home.
variations: She treats the store like her own house — minus the mortgage stress.
Here you may try things on, sit, cool off. You may not take off your shoes, eat a picnic, or yell at the staff.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Pleated Jeans 2026-05-11 sushi-restaurant Kayla roundup; rewritten as 「客人覺得我們是 24 小時待命的 AI」 modern-tech-expectation joke — original framing

zh-tw.content:
客人晚上十一點傳訊：「在嗎？」
隔天早上八點半，他第一通電話打進來：「為什麼昨天沒回我？」
先生，我們是服務業，不是 ChatGPT，
我們會下班、會睡覺、會夢到您罵我們。
您不能 24 小時連線到我，
您家的 Wi-Fi 都不一定 24 小時穩定了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「為什麼沒馬上回」攻擊過的客服與小編同事。AI 都會當機重啟了，何況是領時薪的人。
variations: 客人以為員工是雲端服務，隨時 online、永遠不離線、出錯還不能退費。
您家的 Wi-Fi 都會斷線，憑什麼我們不能下班。

en.content:
Customer texts at 11pm: "You there?"
8:30am, his first call comes in: "Why didn't you reply yesterday?"
Sir, we're a service business. Not ChatGPT.
We clock out, we sleep, we dream about you yelling at us.
You cannot stay connected to me 24/7.
Even your home Wi-Fi isn't connected 24/7.
en.editorNote: Send to customer-support coworkers and social-media managers who got hit with "why didn't you reply instantly?" If AI gets to crash and reboot, an hourly worker definitely does.
variations: Customers think employees are cloud services: always online, never down, no refund on errors.
Your Wi-Fi drops out. Somehow I'm not allowed to clock out.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 方格子 「客人就是欺善怕惡」 essay; rewritten as 「客人對 AI 客服很有耐心，對真人客服沒有」 modern技術倒置 framing — original anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人跟 AI 客服機器人講話，會說「請」、會說「謝謝」、
還會打字打很慢怕它聽不懂。
轉接到真人客服，第一句話：「終於是人了，你給我聽好。」
原來機器人比人類受尊重，
因為機器人不會哭，
客人罵爽就好。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給做客服的同行——尤其是那些被「終於是人了」開頭的電話折磨過的。AI 時代最諷刺的事情：機器人擁有的禮貌，比真人客服還多。
variations: 客人對 AI 講話比對真人客氣，因為他知道 AI 沒有眼淚可以收。
機器人不會委屈，所以客人捨不得對它兇——這是 2026 年最荒謬的禮貌邏輯。

en.content:
When customers talk to the AI chatbot, they say "please," they say "thanks,"
they type slowly in case the bot can't keep up.
Transferred to a human agent, first sentence: "Finally, a real person. Now you listen to me."
Turns out robots get more respect than people,
because robots don't cry,
so the customer can yell guilt-free.
en.editorNote: Send to call-center coworkers — especially anyone who got worn down by a call that opened with "finally, a real person." The most ironic thing about the AI era: the chatbot gets more politeness than the human.
variations: Customers are politer to AI than to humans, because they know AI has no tears to collect.
Robots can't feel hurt, so customers feel bad yelling at them. That's the dumbest politeness logic of 2026.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 心情板 「奧客經典語錄」 roundup; rewritten as 「您知道我是誰嗎？」 self-introduction punchline — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
奧客：「妳知道我是誰嗎？」
我笑著回：「不好意思，這位先生，
我這邊有客戶資料系統可以查，
您要不要報一下您的會員編號？」
奧客愣了三秒：「⋯⋯算了。」
原來「我是誰」這個問題，
他自己也答不出來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「您知道我是誰嗎？」綁架的櫃台同事。會員系統是這個時代最有效的奧客照妖鏡——真正的 VIP 不需要自報名號，他的編號會幫他講話。
variations: 問「你知道我是誰嗎？」的客人，通常就是那個沒有人需要記住的人。
真正的 VIP 不會問你認不認得他，因為他知道系統會幫他簽到。

en.content:
Difficult customer: "Do you know who I am?"
Me, smiling: "No problem, sir.
We have a customer database I can look up.
Would you like to give me your membership number?"
Three seconds of silence: "...Never mind."
Turns out "who I am"
was a question he couldn't answer either.
en.editorNote: Send this to front-desk coworkers recently held hostage by "do you know who I am?" The customer database is this era's best entitled-customer detector — actual VIPs don't announce themselves; their number does it for them.
variations: Customers who ask "do you know who I am?" are usually the ones nobody had to remember.
Real VIPs don't ask if you recognize them. They know the system already checked them in.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Pleated Jeans 2026-05-25 「24 雙 Skechers 分九天到貨」 viral retail story; rewritten as 「客人把店員當宅配 + 個人購物秘書」 dual-role satire — original framing

zh-tw.content:
客人網購了二十四雙鞋，
分九天到貨。
每天我們打電話通知，他每天說「我很忙」。
第十天他終於來了，要求我們當場拆封檢查，
拆到一半說「我趕時間」就走了。
一週後他回來，要求我們把所有鞋子
用紙箱重新打包成他要的尺寸。
先生，您買的是鞋子，
不是把我們當您的私人物流加管家。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給賣場、超商收包裹的同事——客人總以為一筆消費就買斷了店員的時間表。我們收貨、通知、保管、拆封、重新打包，每一項都有實際成本，公司沒收，客人不付，最後變成員工的隱形工時。
variations: 您家樓下有便利商店，您家裡有 PChome 24h，怎麼就是覺得我們店是您的私人倉庫？
客人下單一秒，員工服務九天——這個時薪換算，全台灣沒有公司付得起。

en.content:
She ordered 24 pairs of shoes online.
They arrived over nine days.
We called every day. She said "I'm busy" every day.
Day ten, she finally showed up, demanded we unbox every pair for inspection,
then halfway through said "I'm in a rush" and walked out.
A week later she came back and asked us to repackage everything
into custom-sized boxes she wanted.
Ma'am, you bought shoes —
not a private logistics team plus a personal assistant.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone in retail or convenience-store parcel handling. Customers act like one purchase buys our entire calendar. Receiving, notifying, storing, unboxing, repackaging — every step has a real cost, the company doesn't charge for it, the customer doesn't pay for it, and it all turns into invisible employee overtime.
variations: You have a convenience store downstairs and Amazon Prime at home. Why is our store your personal warehouse?
One second to click "buy." Nine days of staff time to serve. No hourly wage on earth covers that math.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 報導者 2026 餐飲缺工率 28% 報導；rewritten as 「四家店缺一家，那一家剛好就是我」 statistical-fatigue satire — original framing

zh-tw.content:
新聞說：餐飲業缺工率 28%，
每四家店就有一家在缺人。
我心想——
那一家剛好就是我這家。
隔壁那家也是。
再隔壁那家也是。
原來 28% 不是統計，
是公司故意算給投資人看的下限。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我們是團隊」洗腦、結果一個人撐三人份的同事。報告上的缺工率永遠比你眼前的低——因為你眼前那家，就是被當作分母來平均的。
variations: 報告說每四家缺一家，現場感覺每家都缺三個人。
缺工率 28% 是給老闆看的版本，現場版本叫做「今天又只有我一個」。

en.content:
The news says: food-service labor shortage is at 28%.
One in four stores is short-staffed.
I think to myself —
that one is mine.
Next door's also short.
The one after that, also short.
Turns out 28% isn't a statistic.
It's the floor the company shows investors.
en.editorNote: Send to any coworker drinking the "we're a team" Kool-Aid while doing the work of three. The shortage rate on the report is always lower than the one in front of you — because the store in front of you is being averaged into the denominator.
variations: The report says one in four. The floor feels like three short everywhere.
28% is the version for the boss. The floor version is called "it's just me again today."

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 工作板 「服務至上但只能忍氣吞聲？」 thread; rewritten as 「公司的『以客為尊』翻譯成『以員工為墊背』」 word-substitution joke — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
公司的 SOP 第一頁寫著：
「以客為尊」。
翻譯成現場版：
以客人為尊，
以員工為墊背，
以老闆為決策，
以薪水為最後一名。
四個排序裡只有一個跟員工有關，
剛好是最後那一個。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合貼在員工休息室、或印在跟主管討論調薪時的小抄上。SOP 的順序就是公司的價值觀順序——員工排在客人後面、排在老闆後面、排在帳本後面。
variations: 公司的優先順序：客人第一、老闆第二、KPI 第三、員工排在「以上皆是」後面。
「以客為尊」這四個字，公司省下的是員工四個字的尊嚴。

en.content:
Page one of the company SOP says:
"Customer first."
Floor translation:
customer first,
employee as cushion,
boss makes the call,
wage finishes last.
Four rankings. Only one involves the employee —
and it's the last one.
en.editorNote: Tape it in the break room or stash it in your notes for your next raise conversation. The order of the SOP is the order of the company's values — employees rank below customers, below the boss, below the ledger.
variations: Corporate priorities: customer first, boss second, KPI third, employee right after "all of the above."
"Customer first" is two words the company saves by spending two words of employee dignity.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 方格子 《以為有錢就了不起的客人請默默找個地方去死》 review; rewritten as 「有錢買的是商品，買不到我陪您演苦情劇」 transactional-limit framing — original anchor

zh-tw.content:
「我有付錢欸。」
是的，您付了錢。
所以您有那杯咖啡、那份餐、那張收據。
您沒有買到的是——
我陪您演苦情劇的時間、
我裝沒聽到您罵髒話的耐心、
我下班後還在想您那句話的失眠夜。
那些不在發票上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我有付錢」開頭壓過的同事。錢買的東西在收據上，沒買的東西在你心裡——而後者通常比前者貴很多。
variations: 您的發票上列出來的，是您買到的東西；沒列出來的，是您沒買到的東西。員工的耐心剛好在第二類。
「我有付錢」這句話的潛台詞，就是「我以為錢買得到所有東西」——很可惜，沒有。

en.content:
"I'm paying, you know."
Yes. You paid.
So you got the coffee, the meal, the receipt.
What you didn't buy —
my time co-starring in your tragedy,
my patience pretending I didn't hear the cursing,
my sleepless night replaying that thing you said.
None of that is on the receipt.
en.editorNote: Send to any coworker recently crushed by the "but I'm paying" opener. What money buys is on the receipt. What it doesn't buy lives in your head — and that part usually costs much more.
variations: What's on your receipt is what you bought. What's not on it is what you didn't. Employee patience falls in the second category.
"I'm paying" subtext: "I assumed money buys everything." Sorry — it doesn't.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by TwistedSifter 2026-03 supermarket-layout-rage viral story; rewritten as 「客人罵的不是改版，是失控感」 psychological-reframe — original framing

zh-tw.content:
客人為了賣場動線改了、
醬油從第三排移到第七排，
當場罵了我十分鐘。
他罵的不是醬油，
他罵的是這個世界又有一件事不在他控制之中——
而我，剛好是那個世界派來的人。
他要的不是道歉，
是把世界調回他熟悉的樣子。
那個我做不到，我只能默默把醬油送他到第七排。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「店為什麼要改」、「東西為什麼換位置」開頭轟炸過的賣場同事。客人罵的從來不是商品，是他自己對失控的恐懼——我們只是剛好被選中的代表。
variations: 客人罵的不是改版的賣場，是改版的人生；只是人生不在我們的 SOP 裡。
醬油換位置就罵十分鐘的人，通常其他事情也沒有他想像中那麼可控。

en.content:
A customer found out we'd moved the soy sauce
from aisle three to aisle seven
and yelled at me for ten minutes.
He wasn't yelling about soy sauce.
He was yelling about one more thing in the world he can't control —
and I happened to be the world's representative on duty.
He didn't want an apology.
He wanted the world set back the way he remembered it.
That one's not in my power. I just walked him over to aisle seven.
en.editorNote: Send to any supermarket coworker who got chewed out over a remodel or a relocation today. The customer was never yelling at the product — they were yelling at their fear of losing control, and we just happen to be the day's chosen delegate.
variations: He wasn't yelling at the remodeled store. He was yelling at a remodeled life. The remodel isn't on our SOP.
Anyone who yells for ten minutes about soy sauce moving aisles is usually finding everything else just as unmanageable.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the recurring Dcard 工作板 「我跟你們老闆很熟」 name-drop trope; rewritten as a 「真的很熟就不用報名號」 logic-flip — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
客人：「我跟你們老闆很熟喔。」
我：「那真是太好了，
那您要不要直接打給他，
請他幫您破例就好？」
客人：「⋯⋯我沒有他電話。」
原來「很熟」的定義，
是熟到連電話都沒存。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我跟你們老闆很熟」威脅過的同事。真正跟老闆熟的客人，從來不需要對著基層店員報名號——他直接走後門就好。
variations: 會搬出「我跟你們老闆很熟」的人，通常跟老闆的關係，就跟我跟金城武一樣熟。
真的跟老闆熟，會打給老闆；跟老闆不熟，才會威脅店員。

en.content:
Customer: "I know your boss really well, you know."
Me: "Oh, wonderful.
Why don't you just call him
and have him make the exception for you?"
Customer: "...I don't have his number."
Turns out "really well"
means not well enough to save the contact.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone threatened with "I know your boss" this week. Customers who actually know the boss never name-drop to a frontline clerk — they just go around the back.
variations: People who pull out "I know your boss really well" usually know the boss about as well as I know Keanu Reeves.
If you actually knew the boss, you'd call the boss. You only threaten the clerk when you don't.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 心情板 久坐不點餐 / 占位 trope; rewritten as 「客人把咖啡廳當共享辦公室」 modern-remote-work satire — original framing

zh-tw.content:
客人點一杯最便宜的美式，
坐了五個小時。
中間借了三次插座、
問了兩次 Wi-Fi 密碼、
開了一場視訊會議、
還請我幫他把冷氣調強一點。
先生，您買的是一杯咖啡，
不是月租的共享辦公室。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「一杯飲料坐整天」客人占位到翻桌率歸零的咖啡廳同事。我們很歡迎您來，只是這裡是咖啡廳，不是您家書房，也不是您公司沒付的那間會議室。
variations: 點一杯美式坐五小時，這不是消費，這是入住。
咖啡廳的座位是用來喝咖啡的，不是用來辦您整間公司的線上會議。

en.content:
He ordered the cheapest Americano
and sat for five hours.
Borrowed an outlet three times,
asked for the Wi-Fi password twice,
ran a video call,
and asked me to crank up the AC.
Sir, you bought a coffee —
not a monthly membership at a co-working space.
en.editorNote: Send to any cafe coworker whose table turnover hit zero because of the "one drink, all day" guest. You're welcome here — it's just that this is a cafe, not your home office, and not the conference room your company forgot to rent.
variations: One Americano, five hours in the seat. That's not a purchase, that's a check-in.
Cafe seats are for drinking coffee, not for hosting your entire company's video calls.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the recurring 「你這什麼態度」 attitude-accusation trope on Dcard 工作板; rewritten as a mirror-flip where the customer's own tone is the answer — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
客人一進門就摔東西、罵髒話、
用手指著我的鼻子，
然後問我：「你這什麼態度？」
我心裡想：
我的態度，是照著您的態度調出來的。
您給我十分的火，
我還能回您一個微笑，
這已經是我這份薪水做得到的極限。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被「你這什麼態度」三個字壓過的同事。我們的態度從來不是憑空生出來的，是客人遞過來、我們努力接住的那一份——而我們接得，已經比公司付的薪水多太多了。
variations: 客人問「你這什麼態度」的時候，最該照鏡子的，通常是問問題的那個人。
您給的是十分的火，我回的是一個微笑，這個匯率，您其實賺很大。

en.content:
He walked in slamming things, swearing,
pointing a finger in my face,
then asked: "What's with your attitude?"
What I thought:
my attitude is calibrated to match yours.
You hand me a ten out of ten of rage,
and I still hand back a smile —
that's already the limit of what this wage can do.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone flattened by "what's with your attitude?" today. Our attitude never comes from nowhere — it's the one the customer handed over and we tried to catch. And we catch far more than the wage pays for.
variations: When a customer asks "what's with your attitude," the person who most needs a mirror is usually the one asking.
You gave me a ten of rage and I gave back a smile. Honestly, on that exchange rate, you came out ahead.

items.sourceNote: Original warm-solidarity closer extending the file's 互助 register; the 「你今天接住了多少人」 reframe of invisible emotional caretaking is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
今天你笑著幫一個慌張的客人找到他要的東西，
你耐著性子重複講了第三次怎麼操作，
你在最忙的時候還是輕聲說了「不好意思讓您久等」。
沒有人替這些事鼓掌，
但你今天，
默默接住了好多個別人的壞心情。
下班了，
換我請你，把自己也接住。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合下班後傳給服務業的朋友，或自己存著。我們整天在接住別人，常常忘了自己也需要被接住一次——今天就先從對自己溫柔開始。
variations: 你今天接住了那麼多人的情緒，記得也留一隻手接住你自己。
沒有人替服務業鼓掌，那就讓我們自己人，替彼此拍一下手。

en.content:
Today you smiled and helped a flustered customer find what they needed.
You patiently explained how it works for the third time.
Even at your busiest, you still said softly, "Sorry to keep you waiting."
Nobody claps for any of that,
but today, quietly,
you caught so many other people's bad moods before they hit the floor.
Now you're off the clock —
let me catch you for a change.
en.editorNote: Good to send a service-industry friend after a shift, or to save for yourself. We spend all day catching other people and forget we need catching too — so start tonight by being gentle with yourself.
variations: You caught so many people's feelings today. Keep one hand free to catch your own.
Nobody claps for service workers, so let's clap for each other.

items.sourceNote: Extends the Dcard 「奧客經典語錄」 thread — the 「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題」 deflection line; rewritten with a fresh boomerang framing where the customer's own logic returns to them

zh-tw.content:
客人最愛說：「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
奇怪了，東西是您買的、規則是您沒看的、
時間是您自己拖的，
怎麼最後變成只有「我們的問題」？
您這句話的重量，全壓在我一個人的肩膀上，
而我連喊一聲累，都得先說「不好意思」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被「那是你們的問題」這六個字一句話打發掉的同事。客人把問題丟過來的速度，永遠比他承認問題的速度快。
variations: 客人說「那是你們的問題」的時候，通常是在把自己的問題快遞給你，運費還要你出。
所有事情到最後都變成「你們的問題」，那請問我的薪水，是不是也算我自己的問題？

en.content:
The customer's favorite line: "That's your problem, not mine."
Strange — you bought it, you skipped the rules,
you're the one who left it till the last minute,
yet somehow it all becomes "our problem"?
The full weight of that sentence lands on my shoulders alone,
and I can't even say I'm tired without first saying "sorry."
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker brushed off today with "that's your problem." Customers always hand off a problem faster than they'll ever admit owning one.
variations: When a customer says "that's your problem," they're really shipping you their problem — postage due, paid by you.
If everything eventually becomes "your problem," then I guess my paycheck is my problem too?

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the 2026 retail-workforce framing of frontline staff as 「human shock absorbers」 absorbing customer frustration; original 「人體避震器」 vehicle-part metaphor is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
公司說我是第一線。
其實我是人體避震器——
客人路上塞車的氣、被老闆罵的氣、
跟家人吵架的氣，
全都顛到我這裡，由我吸收。
避震器壞了會換新的，
我壞了，公司只說：「下一位。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天替全世界的壞情緒墊背、回家整個人散架的同行。我們不是不會壞，是壞了沒人換、只能自己慢慢修。
variations: 服務業是整台車最便宜、最常壞、卻最不被感謝的那個零件。
客人帶來的不是消費，是他從進門前累積的一整天，由我替他接地。

en.content:
The company calls me the front line.
Really I'm a human shock absorber —
the traffic that cut them off, the boss who chewed them out,
the fight they had at home,
every bump lands on me, and I soak it up.
A worn shock absorber gets replaced.
When I wear out, the company just says: "Next."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who cushions the whole world's bad moods all day and goes home rattling apart. It's not that we don't break — it's that nobody swaps us out, so we patch ourselves up slowly.
variations: Service workers are the cheapest, most replaced, least thanked part on the whole vehicle.
Customers don't bring you a transaction. They bring you the entire day they had before walking in.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「I don't get paid enough for this」 viral-thread format; original 「這個薪水沒包含這一項」 itemized-receipt framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
有些時刻，心裡只會冒出一句話：
「這個薪水，沒有包含這一項。」
被吼、被丟錢、被指著鼻子、
被當成出氣筒——
這些都是公司沒寫進職務說明、
也沒算進時薪裡的隱藏項目。
我領的是基本工資，做的卻是全餐。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合做完一件遠超職責範圍的爛事之後，私訊給最懂你的同事。我們不是不願意做，是這份薪水真的沒買到這麼多。
variations: 服務業的薪水算的是有寫在職缺上的，沒寫的那些，全是佛心免費附贈。
「我這個薪水沒包含被你罵」這句話，我每天在心裡刷三次卡。

en.content:
Some moments, only one line comes to mind:
"This wage does not cover this."
Getting yelled at, having money thrown at me, a finger in my face,
being somebody's punching bag —
none of it is in the job description,
none of it is priced into the hourly rate.
I'm paid for the basics and delivering the full course.
en.editorNote: Good to DM the coworker who gets you, right after you handle something miles outside your job. It's not that we won't do it — this paycheck genuinely didn't buy all that.
variations: The wage covers what's written in the listing. Everything else is a free gift from the goodness of our hearts.
"This pay doesn't include being yelled at by you" — I swipe that card three times a day in my head.

items.sourceNote: Extends the 方格子 「客人欺善怕惡」 register with the 「說話一定要大聲」 volume-as-power trope; original 「音量不等於道理」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
有些客人有個共同的信仰：
講話越大聲，道理就越站得住腳。
他在櫃檯前吼得整間店都聽得到，
以為音量是一種談判籌碼。
其實全店的人只記得一件事——
那位很大聲的客人，
沒有人記得他到底有沒有理。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人用音量壓著打、卻沒辦法回嘴的同事。大聲的人贏的只有當下，輸的是全店對他的印象。
variations: 音量不等於道理，吼得越大聲，通常只代表他手上的牌越爛。
客人以為提高音量是加分，其實全店都在心裡幫他扣分。

en.content:
Some customers share one belief:
the louder you talk, the more right you are.
He yells loud enough for the whole store to hear,
as if volume were a bargaining chip.
But everyone in the store only remembers one thing —
the really loud guy.
Nobody remembers whether he actually had a point.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got steamrolled by sheer volume today with no way to answer back. The loud one wins the moment and loses the whole room's opinion.
variations: Volume isn't an argument. The louder someone gets, the worse the cards in their hand usually are.
He thinks turning up the volume scores points. The whole store is quietly docking them.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「都是他做的，我什麼都不知道」 buck-passing 厭世職場語錄 from the manager/colleague angle into a service-floor scene; original 「最後一線」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
出事的時候，主管說：「我什麼都不知道，是他們現場處理的。」
客訴的時候，公司說：「這是員工個人行為。」
業績好的時候，功勞是團隊的；
出包的時候，責任是我一個人的。
站在最前線的意思是：
好處輪不到我先拿，黑鍋永遠我先背。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近替主管擔了不該擔的責任、卻連一句謝謝都沒收到的同行。最前線聽起來很威風，其實是最先中槍的位置。
variations: 好事是團隊的，壞事是我的，這種數學服務業每天都在算。
站第一線不是榮譽，是出事時離砲口最近的那個位置。

en.content:
When something goes wrong, the manager says: "I had no idea, the floor staff handled it."
When there's a complaint, the company says: "That was the employee's personal conduct."
Good numbers? Credit goes to the team.
A screwup? The blame is mine alone.
Being on the front line means:
I'm never first to the reward, always first to take the fall.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who carried blame that wasn't theirs for a manager lately and didn't even get a thank-you. "Front line" sounds heroic — it's really just the spot that gets hit first.
variations: Good news belongs to the team, bad news belongs to me. Service workers do that math every day.
Front line isn't an honor. It's the position closest to the muzzle when something goes off.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the canonical 「條碼刷不過 = 我猜是免費的吧」 retail in-joke from Keeping's 2026 customer-service jokes roundup; original 「全世界都覺得這句很好笑，只有店員笑不出來」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
商品條碼刷三次都嗶不過，
客人一定會說那句：「那是不是免費的啊？哈哈。」
我也一定會笑。
但這個笑話，全世界覺得好笑，
只有當天第七次聽到的我，笑不出來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被同一個梗笑話招呼的櫃檯同事。客人以為自己很幽默，其實這句話我們一天要陪笑十次。
variations: 客人以為自己是第一個說「免費的吧」的人，其實他是今天的第七個。
服務業最會演的不是道歉，是把聽過一百次的爛笑話陪笑成第一次。

en.content:
The barcode won't scan after three tries,
and the customer always says it: "Guess it's free then, huh?"
And I always laugh.
The whole world finds that joke funny —
just not the version of me hearing it for the seventh time today.
en.editorNote: Send to the cashier coworker greeted by the same joke again today. The customer thinks they're hilarious; we laugh at this one ten times a shift.
variations: The customer thinks they're the first to say "guess it's free." They're actually number seven today.
The best acting in service work isn't the apology. It's laughing at a hundredth-time joke like it's the first.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「retail is hide-and-seek, the customer always finds you」 meme from Pleated-Jeans' 2026 customer-service collection; original 躲廁所 / 補貨間 hiding-spot escalation is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
服務業是一場永遠輸的捉迷藏。
我躲進補貨間，客人找得到。
我躲去倉庫盤點，客人找得到。
我連躲進廁所，門外都有人喊：「請問有人嗎？」
這份工作最神奇的是——
我隨時都能被找到，只有薪水永遠找不到。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每次想喘口氣就被客人精準定位的同行。我們不是不想休息，是這份工作沒有真正的隱身點。
variations: 服務業的躲貓貓只有客人會贏，因為他們有雷達，我們只有制服。
店員可以躲到任何地方，唯獨躲不掉下一個喊「不好意思」的人。

en.content:
Service work is a game of hide-and-seek you always lose.
I hide in the stockroom — they find me.
I hide in the warehouse doing inventory — they find me.
I hide in the bathroom and someone knocks: "Anyone in there?"
The magic of this job:
I can be found anytime — only the paycheck stays hidden.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who gets pinpointed the second they try to catch a breath. It's not that we don't want a break — this job has no real hiding spot.
variations: In service-work hide-and-seek only the customer wins. They have radar; we have a uniform.
A clerk can hide anywhere except from the next person about to say "excuse me."

items.sourceNote: Reframes the canonical 「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題」 奧客 deflection line from the Dcard 心情板 奧客語錄 thread; original 「問題會搬家」 ownership-transfer framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最強的句型：「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
一句話，問題就從他那邊，瞬間搬到我這邊。
是他輸錯密碼、是他沒帶會員卡、是他自己看錯日期，
但只要這句一出，
所有的錯，就都變成我站著該扛的。
服務業最累的不是做事，是替別人收養他不要的問題。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人一句話甩鍋、還得笑著善後的同行。問題會搬家，而且永遠往制服那邊搬。
variations: 客人說「這是你們的問題」的瞬間，問題的戶籍就遷到我名下了。
服務業收的不只是錢，還有一堆客人不肯認領的錯。

en.content:
The customer's most powerful line: "That's your problem, not mine."
One sentence, and the problem teleports from their side to mine.
He typed the wrong password, forgot his card, misread the date —
but the moment he says it,
every mistake becomes the one I'm standing here to carry.
The tiring part of service work isn't the tasks. It's adopting the problems other people refuse to claim.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who got a problem dumped on them in one sentence today and had to clean it up smiling. Problems relocate — always toward whoever's wearing the uniform.
variations: The instant a customer says "that's your problem," the problem changes its legal address to mine.
Service work collects more than money. It collects every mistake customers won't claim.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「working hard or hardly working?」 tired-customer-joke trope from danfe.io's 2026 work-stress humor guide; original 「廢話偵測器」 small-talk-fatigue framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最愛問的廢話前三名：
「很閒喔？」（我手上同時在做四件事）
「你們生意是不是不好？」（現在是離峰時段）
「上班很爽吧，吹冷氣坐著？」（我已經站八小時了）
我們不是不想回，
是回了你也不信，
不如把那口氣留著，撐到下班。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被客人用「你很閒喔」開場的同行。我們的忙不忙，從來不是用客人眼睛看得到的方式在進行。
variations: 客人看到的「很閒」，是我們把忙亂藏在微笑底下的成果。
服務業的閒，是一種演技，不是一種狀態。

en.content:
The customer's top three pointless questions:
"Slow day, huh?" (I'm doing four things at once)
"Business not great?" (it's literally off-peak)
"Must be nice, just sitting in the AC?" (I've been standing for eight hours)
It's not that we don't want to answer.
It's that you won't believe us anyway —
so we save the breath to make it to clock-out.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone greeted with "slow day, huh?" again today. Whether we're busy was never something visible to the customer's eye.
variations: The "slow day" a customer sees is just how well we hid the chaos behind a smile.
In service work, looking idle is a performance, not a state.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 方格子 「客人欺善怕惡」 register through the 「叫你們店長出來」 manager-summoning escalation; original 「店長不是更高一階的我，是同一個被罵的人」 solidarity reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人說：「叫你們店長出來！」
好像店長是傳說中可以滿足所有願望的人。
但客人不知道，
店長出來，講的會是一模一樣的規定，
只是他被罵的時候，
還要分一半的眼神回頭安慰我。
我們不是上下級，
是同一條船上，輪流站在砲口前的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人吵著要找主管、結果店長一起被罵的同行。店長不是更高階的擋箭牌，是跟你一起淋雨的人。
variations: 客人以為店長是大魔王，其實店長只是另一個穿制服、規定背得比較熟的我。
「叫你們店長出來」換來的，通常是兩個人一起被罵，而不是一個人被救。

en.content:
The customer says: "Get me your manager!"
As if the manager were some legend who grants every wish.
What they don't know:
the manager comes out and quotes the exact same policy —
only while getting yelled at,
he still spares half a glance back to reassure me.
We aren't ranks above and below.
We're on the same boat, taking turns standing at the muzzle.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose customer demanded a manager today, only for the manager to get yelled at too. The manager isn't a higher-ranking shield — they're the person standing in the same rain as you.
variations: The customer thinks the manager is the final boss. The manager is just another me in a uniform who memorized the policy better.
"Get me your manager" usually buys two people getting yelled at, not one person getting rescued.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 心情板 「奧客經典語錄」 thread — 「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題」 line; original 「問題分配權」 ownership-redistribution framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最強的一句：「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
翻譯過來是：「我不想負責，但你要負責讓我滿意。」
奧客不是不懂責任，
是太懂了——
懂到知道怎麼把它整包塞給站在櫃檯的你。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天剛被「那是你們的問題」這句話打發掉的同行。責任沒有消失，只是被轉帳到你的情緒帳戶。
variations: 客人說「不是我的問題」的時候，通常已經把問題打包好，貼上你的名字了。
奧客的超能力，是把自己的麻煩瞬間變成你的責任。

en.content:
The customer's ultimate line: "That's your problem, not mine."
Translation: "I refuse to be responsible, but you're responsible for keeping me happy."
Difficult customers don't misunderstand responsibility.
They understand it perfectly —
well enough to pack the whole thing up and hand it to the person at the counter.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone brushed off with "that's your problem" today. The responsibility didn't vanish — it just got wired into your emotional account.
variations: When a customer says "not my problem," they've usually already packed the problem up with your name on the label.
A difficult customer's superpower is turning their own mess into your responsibility in one sentence.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Modern Health 2026 retail-workforce data (can't close a laptop and walk away); original 「沒有關機鍵」 always-on framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
辦公室的人壓力大，可以闔上筆電走人。
我們的壓力，是站在原地、面對下一個排隊的人。
沒有暫停鍵，沒有關機鍵，
只有下一聲「下一位」。
服務業的累，不是做完就結束，
是做完還得馬上補位給下一個。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給覺得「我又不是坐辦公室，怎麼會比較累」被質疑過的同行。累的不是體力，是沒有縫隙的連續性。
variations: 別人下班是關機，我們下班是強制斷電——電量早就見底了。
辦公室的壓力會結束，櫃檯的壓力只會換一個人重來。

en.content:
Office workers can close the laptop and walk away.
Our stress is standing still, facing the next person in line.
No pause button. No power-off.
Just the next "next, please."
Service-industry tired doesn't end when the task ends.
It ends, then immediately refills for the next one.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who's been told "you don't even sit at a desk, how are you tired?" The exhaustion isn't physical — it's the gapless, back-to-back continuity.
variations: Other people clock out by shutting down. We clock out by force-quitting — the battery hit zero hours ago.
Office stress finishes. Counter stress just resets with a new face.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by TVBS 「14種奇葩奧客」 同行心聲 piece; original 「奧客圖鑑」 collector-card framing turning daily abuse into a wry catalogue is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
做服務業久了，會收集到一整本奧客圖鑑：
結帳前甜、結帳後酸的變臉款；
打烊才進門的關門款；
「我朋友比較便宜」的查無此人款。
別人收集公仔，我們收集陰影，
而且還是非自願的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給做久了已經能一眼分類奧客的資深同行。我們的職業病，是把傷害整理成圖鑑才笑得出來。
variations: 服務業的成就系統，是集滿各種奧客類型，獎勵是更厚的黑眼圈。
別人的收藏會增值，我們的奧客圖鑑只會增加離職的念頭。

en.editorNote: Send to the veteran who can sort a difficult customer at a glance. Our occupational hazard is cataloguing the damage just to be able to laugh at it.
variations: Service work's achievement system: collect every customer type. The reward is darker eye circles.
Other people's collections appreciate in value. Our customer bestiary only grows the urge to quit.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 換日線 「款待精神被無限上綱」 critique; original 「以客為尊 ≠ 以客為神」 one-character escalation is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
公司說「以客為尊」，
客人聽成「以客為神」。
尊重是雙向的，崇拜是單向的——
我可以尊重你，
但我沒有義務在你面前燒香。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合做成員工休息室的小卡。以客為尊是服務的底線，不是要員工把客人供起來拜。
variations: 把客人當尊貴的人是專業，把客人當神來拜是失去自己。
以客為尊的「尊」是尊重，不是要員工跪著尊敬。

en.content:
The company says "honor the customer."
The customer hears "worship the customer."
Respect runs both ways. Worship runs one.
I can respect you —
I'm just not obligated to burn incense in front of you.
en.editorNote: Good as a break-room card. Honoring the customer is the baseline of service, not a command to build them a shrine.
variations: Treating a customer as a valued person is professional. Treating them as a deity is losing yourself.
The "honor" in "honor the customer" means respect — not kneeling.

items.sourceNote: Reframes Modern Health 2026 finding that burnout is systemic not personal; original 「不是你抗壓性低，是缺工把三個人的班壓在你身上」 understaffing-reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
你不是抗壓性低。
是這間店本來該排三個人的班，
現在只剩你一個在撐。
累到想哭不是你脆弱，
是一個人扛了三個人的重量，
還被問為什麼撐不住。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近因為缺工被排成一打三、又被主管說「現在年輕人吃不了苦」的同行。撐不住不是你的問題，是排班表的問題。
variations: 缺工不是你的能力問題，是公司把省下來的人事費，換成你的黑眼圈。
一個人做三個人的事，累的時候別怪自己，要怪那兩個沒被補上的缺。

en.content:
You're not weak under pressure.
This store was supposed to schedule three people for this shift.
Now it's just you, holding the floor.
Wanting to cry from exhaustion isn't fragility.
It's carrying three people's weight
and still being asked why you can't keep up.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone scheduled one-against-three by understaffing, then told "young people just can't handle hard work." Not coping isn't your problem — it's the schedule's.
variations: Understaffing isn't a skills gap. It's the company trading the payroll it saved for the dark circles under your eyes.
Doing three people's jobs alone — when you're exhausted, don't blame yourself. Blame the two empty slots nobody filled.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 心情板 「奧客經典語錄」 thread; the 「這是你們的問題不是我的問題」 deflection line rewritten as a one-way ownership transfer is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最愛說：「這是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
奇怪的是，
錯的時候永遠是我們的問題，
對的時候卻又變成他的功勞。
原來『問題』也是看心情分配的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天剛被推鍋的同事。出包了是「你們的問題」，順利了是「我早就說過」，這套邏輯客人練得比我們的 SOP 還熟。
variations: 客人的「不是我的問題」，翻譯成中文是：我不想負責，但我想罵人。
錯的歸我們，對的歸他——這不是服務，是替客人保管所有他不想要的責任。

en.content:
The customer's favorite line: "That's your problem, not mine."
Funny thing —
when it goes wrong, it's always our problem.
When it goes right, it's suddenly their credit.
Turns out "problem" gets handed out based on mood too.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got the blame pinned on them today. Mess up and it's "your problem"; smooth sailing and it's "I told you so." Customers practice that logic harder than we practice our SOP.
variations: "Not my problem" translates to: I don't want to take responsibility, but I do want to yell.
We get the failures, they get the wins — that's not service, it's storing every responsibility the customer doesn't want.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the 2026-05 TwistedSifter story on a worker used as a customer's emotional punching bag; the 「我的工作是賣商品，不是收你的情緒垃圾」 boundary line is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人站在櫃檯前，
把他今天所有的不順，
一句一句倒在我身上。
我想說的是：
我這裡賣的是商品，
不是您情緒的回收站。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被客人當垃圾桶倒情緒、回家整個人空掉的同行。聽客人說話是工作，被當情緒回收站不是。
variations: 客人付的錢買的是杯子，不是把店員當情緒垃圾分類的入場券。
我的耳朵是用來確認您的餐點，不是用來承接您整個禮拜的怨氣。

en.content:
The customer stands at the counter
and pours out everything that went wrong in their day,
one sentence at a time, onto me.
What I want to say is:
I sell products here,
not recycling space for your emotions.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone used as a customer's emotional dumpster lately, who goes home feeling hollowed out. Listening to customers is the job; being their emotional recycling bin isn't.
variations: Their money bought a cup, not an admission ticket to sort their feelings into the clerk.
My ears are here to confirm your order, not to catch a whole week of your resentment.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Modern Health 2026 finding that 40% of retail workers report declining mental health; original 「下班後的我，是白天那個我的二手版本」 secondhand-self image is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
報告說，每十個服務業的人，
就有四個覺得自己的心理狀態在往下掉。
我懂——
白天那個會笑、會應對、會說「好的沒問題」的我，
下班後只剩一個二手版本回家，
而且還是過了保固的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近覺得自己越來越笑不出來的同行。狀態下滑不是你想太多，是四成的人跟你一起在下滑——你只是其中一個誠實說出來的。
variations: 服務業最累的不是站著，是把今天最好的自己留給了客人，剩下的才帶回家。
白天的我是新品，下班的我是展示品，回到家只剩瑕疵品特價出清。

en.content:
The report says four in ten service workers
feel their mental health slipping.
I get it —
the me who smiles, handles things, says "sure, no problem" all day
goes home as a secondhand version of myself.
Warranty already expired.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who's been finding it harder to smile lately. The decline isn't you overthinking — four in ten are sliding right alongside you. You're just one of the honest ones.
variations: The exhausting part isn't standing all day. It's giving the best version of yourself to customers and bringing home what's left.
Morning me is brand new, off-shift me is the floor model, home me is the clearance-rack reject.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 奧客語錄 「叫你們店長出來」 manager-summoning trope; original 「店長出來也是人，不是您的人形出氣筒升級版」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人怒吼：「叫你們店長出來！」
店長出來了，
客人以為自己升級了。
其實只是換一個人，
陪您把同一段不講理，
再聽一次。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人喊「叫店長出來」、後來發現店長也只能跟你一起苦笑的同事。換人聽不會讓無理變有理，只會多累一個人。
variations: 「叫店長出來」不會讓您比較對，只會讓兩個人一起記住您今天的樣子。
客人以為升級客訴對象就會贏，其實只是把不講理重播一次給更累的人聽。

en.content:
The customer roars: "Get me your manager!"
The manager comes out,
and the customer thinks they've leveled up.
Really, it's just a different person
who gets to hear the same unreasonable rant
one more time.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got hit with "get me your manager" today and watched the manager end up sharing the same tired smile. Swapping listeners doesn't make a bad point good — it just exhausts one more person.
variations: "Get me your manager" doesn't make you more right. It just means two people now remember how you acted today.
Escalating the complaint doesn't win anything — it just replays the unreasonableness for someone even more tired.
